title: Spain
subtitle: 1975–today · Transition, Democracy and the Unfinished Nation

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: Constitutional monarchy — from La Transición to today

entries.label: Juan Carlos I — architect of the Transition
entries.hint: Franco designates Juan Carlos as his successor expecting continuity — within two years Juan Carlos dismantles the dictatorship through legal reform from within; his decisive televised speech on 23 February 1981 stops the coup attempt and cements democratic legitimacy.
entries.note: Juan Carlos I's accession speech 22 Nov 1975: BOE 23 Nov 1975. His 23-F speech transcript: RTVE archive (broadcast live, 1:14am, 24 Feb 1981). The 153 declassified 23-F documents (Feb 2026): La Moncloa website — include telephone transcripts. Powell (1996), Juan Carlos of Spain: the standard English biography. He abdicated 18 Jun 2014: BOE 19 Jun 2014.

entries.label: Felipe VI — constitutional king
entries.hint: Felipe VI accedes on his father's abdication — his reign is defined by the 2017 Catalan independence crisis, during which his televised address (3 October 2017) sides explicitly with constitutional order against the referendum; a significant departure from his father's consensual style.
entries.note: Abdication law of Juan Carlos I: BOE 19 Jun 2014. Felipe VI's 3 Oct 2017 speech: RTVE broadcast, transcript on La Moncloa. Constitutionally, the speech's political content was unusual — analysed by constitutional scholars in real time. The reign's constitutional status: documented in BOE throughout.

entries.label: Juan Carlos I abdicates — scandal and the crown's political necessity
entries.hint: Juan Carlos I's abdication is forced by an accumulation of royal scandals — his elephant-hunting trip in Botswana (April 2012, during Spain's worst unemployment crisis), the Nóos corruption case involving his son-in-law Iñaki Urdangarin and his daughter Infanta Cristina, and his relationship with Corinna zu Sayn-Wittgenstein; Felipe VI's accession is a political necessity to preserve the institution.
entries.note: Juan Carlos I's abdication announcement 2 Jun 2014: BOE. The Botswana hunting trip April 2012: documented in press — Juan Carlos hospitalised while on safari. The Nóos case: Infanta Cristina's eventual acquittal but Urdangarin convicted (2017): BOE and court records. Juan Carlos I's self-imposed exile to Abu Dhabi (August 2020) following money-laundering investigations: BOE announcement. Pradera (2014), Corrupción y política: contextualises the scandals. Felipe VI's accession 19 Jun 2014: BOE.

entries.label: Adolfo Suárez — first democratic prime minister
entries.hint: Suárez, a former Franco official, is the paradox of the Transition: a man of the regime who dismantles it from within; his Ley para la Reforma Política (1977) transforms the Francoist Cortes into a democratic parliament through a self-abolishing vote.
entries.note: Suárez's appointment by Juan Carlos: BOE 5 Jul 1976. Ley para la Reforma Política, ratified by Francoist Cortes 18 Nov 1976: BOE 5 Jan 1977. Referendum approving the LRP, 15 Dec 1976: Interior Ministry results. Powell (1996). The Cortes voting itself out of existence: documented in Diario de Sesiones. Suárez resigned Jan 1981, weeks before the coup attempt.

entries.label: Felipe González — Socialist transformation
entries.hint: González wins an absolute majority in 1982 — the Socialists govern for 14 years, overseeing EU accession, NATO integration, economic modernisation and the welfare state's expansion; his government ends in corruption scandals (GAL affair, Filesa case).
entries.note: Appointment: BOE 3 Dec 1982. Re-elections: 1986, 1989, 1993 (minority). EU accession 1986: under his government. GAL affair (state-sponsored anti-ETA death squads): documented in Audiencia Nacional records and Diario 16 investigative reporting 1988–1995. Resignation 1996: BOE 6 May 1996. Gillespie (1989), The Spanish Socialist Party: the standard academic study.

entries.label: José María Aznar — conservative modernisation
entries.hint: Aznar wins in 1996 on economic reform and anti-corruption — oversees Spain's entry into the euro (1999), an economic boom, and controversial support for the Iraq War (2003); the mishandling of the 11-M bombings (blaming ETA) costs his party the election three days later.
entries.note: Appointment: BOE 6 May 1996. Spain's euro entry 1 Jan 1999: BOE. Iraq War support: Aznar's signature on the 'Letter of Eight' (Wall Street Journal, 30 Jan 2003) — the primary document. The 20M anti-war protest 15 Feb 2003: Interior Ministry estimates. 11-M and electoral defeat: Interior Ministry election results 14 Mar 2004.

entries.label: José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero — social liberalism
entries.hint: Zapatero withdraws troops from Iraq within days of taking office, legalises same-sex marriage (2005), passes the Historical Memory Law (2007) and gender violence law — then presides over the catastrophic 2008 crash and austerity; he declines to seek a third term.
entries.note: Appointment: BOE 18 Apr 2004. Iraq withdrawal order: BOE Apr 2004. Same-sex marriage: Ley 13/2005, BOE 2 Jul 2005. Historical Memory Law: BOE 27 Dec 2007. The 2010 austerity decree: BOE 12 May 2010. Resignation: announced Jan 2011, new elections Nov 2011. Público and El País archives document the government's record in real time.

entries.label: Mariano Rajoy — austerity and the Catalan crisis
entries.hint: Rajoy governs through the deepest austerity in Spanish democratic history — unemployment peaks at 27%; his government falls in a no-confidence motion over corruption (Gürtel case), the first such dismissal in Spanish democratic history.
entries.note: Appointment: BOE 22 Dec 2011. Austerity measures: multiple BOE decrees 2012–2013. Gürtel corruption case: Audiencia Nacional records, sentence 2018. No-confidence motion (moción de censura) 1 Jun 2018: Diario de Sesiones del Congreso — the first successful no-confidence vote in Spanish democratic history. The Catalan referendum and Article 155 activation: BOE Oct 2017.

entries.label: Pedro Sánchez — minority government and fragmentation
entries.hint: Sánchez reaches power through the no-confidence vote against Rajoy — the first socialist PM without winning an election; governs through four elections in four years (2019×2, 2023), COVID-19, the Catalan amnesty law controversy, and the fragmentation of the party system.
entries.note: Appointment after no-confidence: BOE 2 Jun 2018. Re-elected Jan 2020 (coalition with Unidas Podemos — first coalition government in Spanish democratic history): BOE 8 Jan 2020. 2023 election results: Interior Ministry — PSOE won fewer seats than PP but formed government with Junts support and amnesty law. Amnesty Law: BOE May 2024. The 153 declassified 23-F documents (Feb 2026): announced by Sánchez as 'settling a historical debt' — La Moncloa press release.

entries.label: Leopoldo Calvo-Sotelo — the PM of Spain's NATO accession
entries.hint: Leopoldo Calvo-Sotelo succeeds Suárez as PM on 26 February 1981 — days after the 23-F coup attempt; he guides Spain into NATO (May 1982) over massive public opposition; his brief 22-month government manages the consolidation of democracy in its most fragile post-coup moment; his UCD coalition collapses at the 1982 elections giving González a historic majority.
entries.note: Calvo-Sotelo's investiture 26 Feb 1981: Congreso de los Diputados records — he was confirmed during the occupation of parliament by Tejero's guardia civil; the vote completed under coup conditions. Spain's NATO accession (30 May 1982): NATO archives. The 1982 election results: Ministerio del Interior — PSOE wins 202 of 350 seats (the largest majority in Spanish democratic history). Powell (2001), España en democracia 1975–2000: the standard study.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: ETA terrorism, the 23-F coup attempt and the 11-M bombings

entries.label: ETA — from political assassinations to dissolution (1975–2018)
entries.hint: ETA (Euskadi Ta Askatasuna) carries out its most intense campaign after Franco's death — assassinating Prime Minister Carrero Blanco (1973, see 1900–1975 folio), Admiral Carrero's successor Arias Navarro's cabinet candidate Luis Carrero (1975), Prime Minister Adolfo Suárez's military aide (1979), and 21 soldiers in Hipercor (1987); ETA declares a unilateral permanent ceasefire on 20 October 2011 and formally dissolves itself on 2 May 2018.
entries.note: ETA's operations under democracy: AHN (Audiencia Nacional terrorism cases). Key attacks: Atocha massacre (1977) — by ultra-right, not ETA; Hipercor bombing 19 Jun 1987 (21 dead, 45 injured, documented); Admiral Carrero Blanco 1973 (see 1900–1975 folio). ETA's total victims 1959–2010: approximately 829 killed — documented by the Asociación de Víctimas del Terrorismo and the COVITE database. The 20 Oct 2011 ceasefire: International Contact Group, documented. Dissolution 2 May 2018: public statement. Llera Ramo (1994), 'ETA: From Secret Army to Social Movement': the standard political science study.

entries.label: 23-F — attempted coup in parliament, 1981
entries.loc: Madrid (Congress of Deputies); Valencia (tanks in streets)
entries.hint: Lt Col Antonio Tejero storms the Congress of Deputies with 200 Civil Guards — the entire government is held hostage for 18 hours; Juan Carlos's televised address at 1:14am defeats the coup; the events are captured on television and radio.
entries.note: EXCEPTIONAL ANCHOR: RTVE footage (extant, broadcast live) — Tejero entering Congress captured on parliamentary cameras. King's speech broadcast 1:14am, 24 Feb 1981: RTVE archive. 153 declassified documents (25 Feb 2026): La Moncloa — include transcripts of telephone conversations between military commanders (Euronews, 25 Feb 2026). Tejero convicted 1982: Tribunal Supremo records.

entries.label: Batasuna banned — the political arm of ETA outlawed (2003)
entries.hint: The Supreme Court bans Batasuna on 27 March 2003 under the Ley Orgánica de Partidos Políticos (2002) — the first time a political party has been banned in democratic Spain; the ruling holds that Batasuna is ETA's political arm; it creates a significant constitutional precedent about the limits of political pluralism.
entries.note: Supreme Court ruling 27 Mar 2003: BOE 28 Mar 2003. The Ley Orgánica de Partidos Políticos 27 Jun 2002: BOE — the enabling legislation. European Court of Human Rights (2009): upholds the ban (Herri Batasuna v Spain, App. 25803/04). Sáiz Valdivieso (2004), Batasuna es ETA: the prosecutorial argument. Casanova (2014), The Spanish Republic and Civil War: contextualises Basque political history. The ban triggers a cycle of successor parties (Herri Batasuna → Batasuna → Sortu) all of which are also challenged legally.

entries.label: 11-M Madrid train bombings — 2004
entries.loc: Madrid (Atocha station; Téllez street; Pozo station)
entries.hint: Al-Qaeda-linked bombers detonate ten improvised explosive devices on four commuter trains — 193 killed, 2,000 injured; the most deadly terrorist attack in Spanish history; the government's initial false attribution to ETA (three days before the general election) is considered a factor in its electoral defeat.
entries.note: Judicial investigation: Audiencia Nacional, Sumario 20/2004 — the trial records are public and extensive. Commission of Inquiry report (2005): Congreso de los Diputados archive. The misattribution to ETA: documented in real-time press records and government communications (AHN). The election results 14 Mar 2004: Interior Ministry archive. 192 victims (one died later): official memorial registry.

entries.label: Catalan independence crisis — 2017 referendum
entries.hint: The Catalan government holds an independence referendum declared illegal by the Constitutional Court — 2.3 million vote (90% yes, 43% turnout); Spanish police violently block polling stations; the Catalan president Puigdemont flees to Belgium; the crisis remains unresolved.
entries.note: Referendum results: Generalitat de Catalunya, 1 Oct 2017. Constitutional Court ruling (ATC 24/2017): declaring the referendum illegal. Puigdemont's exile in Belgium: Belgian court records. The UN Human Rights Committee ruling (2023): found Spain violated Puigdemont's right to stand for election. Felipe VI's 3 Oct 2017 speech: RTVE archive. The 2021 pardons of imprisoned leaders: BOE 22 Jun 2021.

entries.label: ETA dissolves — end of 59-year armed campaign (2018)
entries.hint: ETA formally dissolves all its structures on 2 May 2018 — ending a 59-year armed campaign that killed approximately 829 people and wounded thousands; it comes seven years after the 2011 permanent ceasefire; the Basque peace process is documented as the most successful European de-radicalisation of a terrorist organisation since the IRA.
entries.note: ETA's dissolution statement 2 May 2018: published in full. The 829 victims: COVITE (Colectivo de Víctimas del Terrorismo) database — the most rigorous count. Muro (2008), Ethnicity and Violence: The Case of Radical Basque Nationalism: the standard political science study. Alonso (2011), Leaving Terrorism Behind: Individual and Collective Disengagement: analyses the demobilisation process. The Glencree Centre (Ireland) facilitated some dialogue — documented.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: The Constitution of 1978 and the legal architecture of democracy

entries.label: Pactos de la Moncloa — the economic consensus
entries.hint: All major political parties sign the Moncloa Pacts — agreeing wage restraint, political reform, and economic stabilisation simultaneously; the pacts are the economic foundation of the Transition, preventing the inflation-triggered crisis that derailed other democratisations.
entries.note: Text of the Moncloa Pacts: BOE 27 Oct 1977. Signed by 7 parties including PCE (Communist) and AP (conservative): documented. Fuentes Quintana (1983), Economía y política en la Transición. The pacts as economic model: cited in international democratic transition literature (O'Donnell & Schmitter 1986).

entries.label: Constitution of 1978 — the Magna Carta of the Transition
entries.hint: The 1978 Constitution is approved by referendum (88.5% in favour, 67.1% turnout) — establishing parliamentary monarchy, regional autonomy, and a rights framework; it is the product of consenso (political consensus) across parties including the Communist Party.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: BOE 29 Dec 1978 — the full constitutional text. Referendum result (6 Dec 1978): Interior Ministry official results. The constitutional drafting process (los padres de la Constitución): documented in the Cortes archives, Diario de Sesiones 1977–78. The Communist Party's role: Santiago Carrillo's PCE participated in drafting — its inclusion was itself a political act. Peces-Barba (2003), La elaboración de la Constitución de 1978.

entries.label: Repeal of Ley de Peligrosidad Social (1979) — CATEGORICAL SHIFT
entries.hint: The Ley de Peligrosidad y Rehabilitación Social (1970) is amended in December 1978 to remove homosexuality as a 'social danger' — completing the legal shift from criminalisation to constitutional equality; the amendment passes during the same month as the Constitution's ratification; the CATEGORICAL SHIFT documented in the 1900–1975 folio is here reversed.
entries.note: Amendment to Ley de Peligrosidad: BOE 26 Dec 1978. The Constitution of 1978 (Article 14 — equality before the law): ratified by referendum 6 Dec 1978 — the same month. Olmeda (2004), El látigo y la pluma: documents the transition from criminalisation to rights. The full repeal of the 1970 law: Ley Orgánica 10/1995 (Penal Code). The arc from lv2 in the 1900–1975 folio to this entry documents the complete CATEGORICAL SHIFT.

entries.label: State of Autonomies — 17 regions self-governing
entries.hint: The 1978 Constitution creates the Estado de las Autonomías — 17 autonomous communities each with their own parliament and government; the Basque Country and Catalonia gain the most extensive powers; the structure is a deliberate ambiguity about whether Spain is federal or unitary.
entries.note: Basque Statute of Autonomy (Estatuto de Gernika): BOE 22 Dec 1979. Catalan Statute (Estatut de Sau): BOE 18 Dec 1979. The subsequent statutes for other regions: BOE throughout 1981–83. The constitutional ambiguity: Morán (2009) on the 'state of autonomies' as an unfinished settlement. The 17 autonomías: documented in Ministerio de las Administraciones Públicas records.

entries.label: GAL affair — state terrorism and Spain's worst constitutional scandal
entries.hint: The GAL (Grupos Antiterroristas de Liberación) are state-sponsored death squads operated by high-ranking Spanish Interior Ministry officials — they conduct assassinations, kidnappings and torture against suspected ETA members in France (1983–1987); the exposure and judicial process leads to criminal convictions of a former Interior Minister (José Barrionuevo) and a Secretary of State; the worst constitutional scandal of Spain's democratic era.
entries.note: The GAL operations 1983–1987: documented in judicial proceedings. The 1997 Supreme Court convictions: published in BOE — José Barrionuevo (Interior Minister) and Rafael Vera (Secretary of State) convicted of kidnapping. Woodworth (2001), Dirty War, Clean Hands: ETA, the GAL and Spanish Democracy: the standard English study using court records. The GAL's 28 victims: documented. The scandal's political impact: directly contributes to the PSOE's 1996 election defeat. This is Spain's Watergate equivalent.

entries.label: Abortion legislation arc — from crime to right (1985–2023)
entries.hint: Spain's abortion law evolves across four decades: partial decriminalisation in three circumstances (1985, PSOE); a right-to-choose law up to 14 weeks (2010, Zapatero); the PP attempts to roll back to 1985 (Gallardón plan, withdrawn 2014); a new law restoring and strengthening the 2010 framework (2023); the arc documents how reproductive rights became a proxy for Spain's culture war.
entries.note: Ley Orgánica 9/1985 (partial decriminalisation): BOE 12 Jul 1985. Ley Orgánica 2/2010 (up to 14 weeks): BOE 4 Mar 2010. The Gallardón withdrawal 2014: documented in press (El País 23 Sep 2014). Ley Orgánica 1/2023 (restored and strengthened): BOE 2 Mar 2023. Constitutional Court's validation of the 1985 law (STC 53/1985): BOE. Nash (1991), 'Pronatalism and Motherhood in Franco's Spain': contextualises the pre-democratic baseline.

entries.label: Ley de Dependencia (2006) — Spain's welfare state completes
entries.hint: The Ley de Dependencia (14 December 2006) creates the first universal entitlement to social care in Spain — the right to professional care for the elderly and disabled regardless of income; the single most significant expansion of the Spanish welfare state since 1978; implementation is chronically underfunded but the legal right is established.
entries.note: Ley 39/2006 de Promoción de la Autonomía Personal y Atención a las personas en situación de dependencia: BOE 15 Dec 2006. The parliamentary debate: Congreso de los Diputados, Diario de Sesiones. The SAAD (Sistema para la Autonomía y Atención a la Dependencia) registry: IMSERSO annual reports — documents the implementation gap. Rodríguez Cabrero (2011), The Long and Winding Road of Advance of the Spanish Welfare State: the standard study.

entries.label: Historical Memory Law — exhumations and recognition
entries.hint: The 2007 Ley de Memoria Histórica mandates a mass graves programme, removes Francoist symbols from public space, and recognises victims of the Civil War and dictatorship — the 2022 Democratic Memory Law strengthens it; both laws are contested politically.
entries.note: Ley 52/2007 de Memoria Histórica: BOE 27 Dec 2007. Ley 20/2022 de Memoria Democrática: BOE 20 Oct 2022. Mass graves database: Ministerio de Presidencia, online. Centro Documental de la Memoria Histórica (Salamanca): the primary archive. The law's political contestation: documented in parliamentary debates (Diario de Sesiones). The PP government's suspension of funding 2011–2015: documented in budget records.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Secularisation, the Church's retreat and plural religious Spain

entries.label: Church-State separation in the 1978 Constitution
entries.hint: Article 16 of the 1978 Constitution declares Spain a non-confessional state while acknowledging the 'special relations' with the Catholic Church — a carefully negotiated formula that ends National Catholicism without confronting the Church directly.
entries.note: Constitution Article 16: BOE 29 Dec 1978. The 1979 Agreements between Spain and the Holy See: BOE 15 Dec 1979 — replace the 1953 Concordat with four partial agreements on legal status, education, military service and economic affairs. The secularisation data: INE (Instituto Nacional de Estadística) surveys document the rapid decline in Mass attendance from ~70% (1975) to ~15% (2020). This is a documented CATEGORICAL SHIFT in how the state defines itself.

entries.label: Muslim community and the Islamic Council of Spain
entries.hint: The 1992 Cooperation Agreement between the Spanish state and the Islamic Commission of Spain formally recognises Islam — reflecting the arrival of significant Muslim immigrant communities; Ceuta and Melilla on the African coast have majority-Muslim populations predating the agreements.
entries.note: Ley 26/1992 (Islamic Cooperation Agreement): BOE 12 Nov 1992. The CIE (Comisión Islámica de España): formally constituted 1992. INE data on Muslim population: from ~150,000 (1990) to ~2 million (2020). The pre-existing Muslim community in Ceuta and Melilla: documented in local civil registry records from the 19th century. Moreras (1999), Musulmanes en Barcelona: the first major sociological study.

entries.label: Canonisation of Escrivá (2002) — Opus Dei's global moment
entries.loc: Rome (St Peter's Square); Pamplona (Opus Dei HQ)
entries.hint: John Paul II canonises Josemaría Escrivá de Balaguer on 6 October 2002 — the fastest canonisation in modern Catholic history (27 years after death); 300,000 attend in Rome; it marks Opus Dei's global institutional peak; in Spain the canonisation is politically charged given Opus Dei's role in the Francoist technocracy; the largest global religious export in Spanish history.
entries.note: Canonisation decree 6 Oct 2002: Vatican records. The speed of canonisation: beatified 1992, canonised 2002 — 27 years after death (1975), against the usual 50-year minimum; the acceleration documented in Vatican procedural records. Opus Dei membership (c.90,000 globally, 2002): institutional figures. Allen (2005), Opus Dei: An Objective Look Behind the Myths: the standard English study. Escrivá's Camino (1939): the primary text — over 4.5 million copies in 40 languages.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: La Movida, cinema, architecture and the cultural explosion

entries.label: Chillida — El Peine del Viento and democratic public space
entries.hint: Eduardo Chillida's El Peine del Viento (The Comb of the Wind, 1977) — three massive iron sculptures anchored into the rocky Basque coast at San Sebastián — becomes the defining symbol of Spain's democratic opening; its integration of heavy industrial ironwork with untamed natural geography expresses a politics of freedom and rootedness that resonates beyond aesthetics.
entries.note: El Peine del Viento (1977): in situ, Paseo del Ondarreta, San Sebastián. Architect: Luis Peña Ganchegui (the plaza setting). Chillida's own account of the work's conception: Chillida (1998), Escritos. The 1977 date: installed during the Transition — the political timing deliberate. Kosme de Barañano (1991), Chillida: the standard monograph. The Guggenheim Bilbao has a major Chillida collection. The work's political resonance in the Basque Country in 1977: documented in contemporary press.

entries.label: Pedro Almodóvar and La Movida Madrileña
entries.hint: La Movida — the creative explosion following Franco's death — produces Almodóvar's first films (Pepi, Luci, Bom, 1980; What Have I Done to Deserve This?, 1984), Andy Warhol's visits to Madrid, and a deliberate transgression of Francoist repression through sexual and artistic freedom.
entries.note: Almodóvar's Pepi, Luci, Bom (1980): first film, made for 500,000 pesetas — production records at El Deseo SA. All About My Mother (1999), Todo sobre mi madre: won the Oscar for Best Foreign Film — Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences records. The Movida's documentation: Ministerio de Cultura archives, press coverage in El País (first edition 4 May 1976 — itself a primary source of the Transition).

entries.label: Camilo José Cela — Spain's Nobel in Literature (1989)
entries.hint: Camilo José Cela wins the Nobel Prize in Literature on 19 October 1989 — the only Spanish writer of the democratic era to receive it; his La Familia de Pascual Duarte (1942, published clandestinely) and La Colmena (1951, published in Buenos Aires due to censorship) are the foundational texts of postwar Spanish realism; the Nobel crowns a career that spans dictatorship and democracy.
entries.note: Nobel Prize announcement 19 Oct 1989: Nobel Foundation records. La familia de Pascual Duarte (1942): published clandestinely (the Francoists banned it within weeks) — BNE. La Colmena (1951): published Buenos Aires (Emecé) due to Spanish censorship — first Spanish edition 1966 (BNE). The Nobel lecture (Stockholm, 8 Dec 1989): Nobel Foundation archive. Ilie (1980), Literature and Inner Exile: contextualises Cela within Francoist culture. Cela's subsequent scandal (plagiarism claims): documented — does not diminish the Nobel's historical significance.

entries.label: Ferran Adrià and elBulli — gastronomy at Documenta 12
entries.hint: Ferran Adrià's elBulli (Roses, Girona) is voted the world's best restaurant five times — his molecular gastronomy and deconstructive techniques redefine cooking as conceptual art; in 2007 he is the first chef officially invited to participate in Documenta 12 in Kassel (the world's leading contemporary art exhibition), where elBulli is designated Pavilion G — the only non-physical pavilion.
entries.note: Documenta 12 (2007): catalogue and documentation — Documenta Archive, Kassel. Adrià's designation as Pavilion G: documented in the official Documenta 12 catalogue (2007). The five 'world's best restaurant' awards: Restaurant Magazine rankings 2002, 2006, 2007, 2008, 2009 — documented. elBulli Foundation (since 2011): Roses, Girona. Adrià's El Bulli: 1998–2002 (published 2002): the definitive documentation of his technique. The Documenta invitation: analysed in Marian Dobrescu, 'Can Gastronomy Be Art?' (2009) — the precedent-setting nature of the invitation.

entries.label: Guggenheim Bilbao — the titanium catalyst
entries.hint: Frank Gehry's Guggenheim Museum Bilbao opens in 1997 — the titanium-clad building transforms a post-industrial waterfront city and triggers the 'Bilbao effect' concept in urban regeneration; the museum receives 1 million visitors in its first year.
entries.note: Extant (Museo Guggenheim Bilbao). Opening 18 Oct 1997: contemporary press records. Construction cost €84 million: Guggenheim Foundation records. The 'Bilbao effect': Plaza et al. (2003), JAPA 69(1) — the academic term coined here. The museum's visitor statistics: Guggenheim annual reports. Ibelings (1998), Supermodernism: the architectural context.

entries.label: Rosalía — flamenco fusion and global reach
entries.hint: Rosalía Vila Tobella's album El Mal Querer (2018) fuses flamenco with electronic production — winning the Latin Grammy for Best Fusion/Urban Interpretation and the Grammy for Best Latin/Urban Album; the most internationally successful Spanish musical artist since Julio Iglesias.
entries.note: El Mal Querer (2018): Sony Music. Latin Grammy winner 2018 (documented). Grammy Award winner 2023: Recording Academy records. The album's flamenco sources: based on a 13th-century Occitan novel Flamenca — the literary source documented in liner notes. Rosalía's conservatory training: Escola Superior de Música de Catalunya records. The album is currently the most studied recent work of Spanish popular music in academic literature.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Democracy, nationalism and the contested memory of the past

entries.label: Transition model — Spain as democratic template
entries.hint: Spain's Transition becomes the global reference model for democratic transitions — O'Donnell and Schmitter cite it in their foundational 1986 study; the consenso approach (negotiation rather than rupture) is exported as a model for Eastern Europe, Latin America and South Africa.
entries.note: O'Donnell & Schmitter (1986), Transitions from Authoritarian Rule: Tentative Conclusions — the foundational comparative politics text, cites Spain repeatedly. Gunther, Sani & Shabad (1986), Spain after Franco: the first systematic academic study. The model's influence on South Africa's transition: Asmal (1994) explicitly references the Spanish pactos. The Transition's limitations — the 'pact of silence' on the past — are the subject of the Historical Memory debates.

entries.label: El País — newspaper of the Transition
entries.hint: El País is founded on 4 May 1976 — the first major liberal newspaper of the Transition; its first edition is itself a primary source for the period; its archives document the entire democratic era in real time.
entries.note: First edition: 4 May 1976 — BNE and El País archive. Circulation: 450,000 daily at its peak (1990s). The paper's role in the Transition: Seoane & Sueiro (2004), Una historia de El País. The paper's archives: digitised from 1976 — the fullest newspaper record of democratic Spain. Note: El País is owned by the PRISA group — its editorial line (centrist-left) must be accounted for in using it as a primary source.

entries.label: Pacto de olvido — the politics of transitional forgetting
entries.hint: The 'pact of forgetting' (pacto de olvido) — the deliberate decision not to prosecute Francoist crimes during the Transition — is never formally agreed but is politically enacted; the amnesty law of 1977 immunises both sides; Paloma Aguilar's research (1996) and Habermas's interventions frame the international debate; the Historical Memory Law (2007) and the Democratic Memory Law (2022) are the belated institutional responses.
entries.note: Amnesty Law (Ley 46/1977, 15 Oct 1977): BOE — the legal instrument of the pact. Aguilar Fernández (1996), Memoria y olvido de la Guerra Civil española: the foundational academic study — based on survey data and political archives. Habermas's interventions on transitional justice: documented in academic collections. The UN Special Rapporteur's criticism of the amnesty law (Pablo de Greiff, 2014): UN document A/HRC/27/56/Add.1. Reyes Mate (2008), La herencia del olvido: the philosophical critique.

entries.label: Javier Muguerza — the ethics of dissent and democratic reason
entries.hint: Javier Muguerza's Desde la perplejidad (1990) is the foundational text of Spanish democratic moral philosophy — his theory of 'dissent' argues that the individual's refusal of unjust norms is the most basic ethical act in a democracy; it gives the Transition generation a philosophical framework for civic responsibility that is neither Marxist nor Catholic.
entries.note: Desde la perplejidad: ensayos sobre la ética, la razón y el diálogo (1990): Fondo de Cultura Económica — BNE. Muguerza's earlier La razón sin esperanza (1977): the formative text written in the final Franco years. His position at the CSIC (Instituto de Filosofía): documented in CSIC records. Cerezo Galán (ed.) (1996), El mal: irradiaciones y paradojas: the collaborative volume documenting the Madrid school of ethics. Muguerza (2009), Ética y ciudadanía: the mature synthesis.

entries.label: Victoria Camps — civic virtue and the ethics of democratic life
entries.hint: Victoria Camps's Virtudes públicas (1990) argues that democracy requires not just rights and laws but civic virtues — honesty, tolerance, responsibility; her work on feminist ethics and public ethics becomes the intellectual foundation for Spain's equality legislation; she chairs the Bioethics Committee of Spain (1995–2014), making her the most institutionally influential philosopher of the democratic era.
entries.note: Virtudes públicas (1990): Espasa-Calpe — BNE. Camps's feminist ethics: La voluntad de vivir (2005). Her role as president of the Comité de Bioética de España (1995–2014): BOE appointment records. Cortina & Martínez (1996), Ética: the textbook that introduces this framework to a generation of Spanish secondary school students — documented in Ministry of Education curriculum records. The connection to the 2004 gender violence law and 2007 equality law: explicitly acknowledged in parliamentary debate records.

entries.label: Javier Marías — literature as philosophy of memory and moral witness
entries.hint: Javier Marías's Tu rostro mañana trilogy (2002–2007) and Corazón tan blanco (1992) develop a sustained literary-philosophical investigation of memory, moral complicity and the knowledge we choose not to act on — directly confronting Spain's unprocessed Civil War inheritance; elected to the Real Academia Española (2008); refused the National Prize for Literature twice on principle.
entries.note: Corazón tan blanco (1992): Editorial Anagrama — BNE; translated into 40 languages. Tu rostro mañana (3 vols, 2002–2007): Alfaguara. Marías's election to the Real Academia Española (2008, chair k): documented in RAE records. His refusals of the National Prize: documented in press (El País 2012). Wood (2012), The Nearest Thing to Life: places Marías in the European philosophical-novel tradition. His death 11 Sep 2022: documented — he died on the anniversary of the Chilean coup, a date he had written about.

entries.label: Santos Juliá and the historians of memory — writing Spain's contested past
entries.hint: Santos Juliá's Víctimas de la guerra civil (1999) and subsequent work establishes rigorous historical methodology for Spain's Civil War memory — documenting both Republican and Nationalist violence with equal evidential standards; his critique of the 'culture of memory' (2003 essay) argues that Spain already had a functional democratic reckoning before the memory laws; the most important counter-voice to the pacto de olvido debate.
entries.note: Víctimas de la guerra civil (1999): Temas de Hoy — BNE. Juliá's 'Echar al olvido: memoria y amnistía en la transición' (Claves de Razón Práctica, 2003): the key intervention in the memory debate. His Historia de las dos Españas (2004): Taurus. His role at El País: weekly column documented in the archive. Aguilar Fernández (2008), Políticas de la memoria y memorias de la política: the response from the opposing school. The juliá-Aguilar debate: the structuring intellectual controversy of democratic Spanish historiography.

entries.label: 15-M Indignados movement — Puerta del Sol occupied
entries.hint: Hundreds of thousands occupy central squares across Spain on 15 May 2011 — protesting austerity, political corruption and the two-party system; the Puerta del Sol camp lasts weeks; the movement directly inspires Podemos (2014) and later Occupy Wall Street.
entries.note: The 15-M manifesto (¡Democracia Real YA!): published in full, archived at BNE digital. Turnout estimates: Interior Ministry (ranging 130,000–2,000,000+ — dispute itself is significant). The camp in Sol: documented in El País and El Mundo from 15 May 2011. Toret (2013), Tecnopolítica: the first academic study. The connection to Podemos founding: Iglesias (2015), Politics in a Time of Crisis — Podemos leader's own account. Castells (2012), Networks of Outrage and Hope: places 15-M in global comparative context.

entries.label: Podemos and the end of bipartidismo — 2014–2015
entries.hint: Podemos is founded at the Complutense University on 16 January 2014 — within four months it wins 5 MEPs in the European elections; in December 2015 it enters the Congress of Deputies with 69 seats, ending the PP-PSOE duopoly that had governed Spain since 1982.
entries.note: Founding assembly 16 Jan 2014: documented in El País, Público (17 Jan 2014). European election results 25 May 2014: Interior Ministry — Podemos 7.98%, 5 MEPs. General election 20 Dec 2015: Interior Ministry — PP 123 seats, PSOE 90, Podemos 69, Ciudadanos 40 (first four-party parliament). Vox's first seats (Dec 2019): 52 deputies — Interior Ministry. This is a documented CATEGORICAL SHIFT: from bipartidismo to multi-party fragmentation in one electoral cycle.

entries.label: Vox — Spain's right-wing populism ends Spanish exceptionalism
entries.hint: Vox's breakthrough in the Andalusian regional elections (December 2018) ends Spain's post-Franco exceptionalism as the only major Western European democracy without a prominent far-right party; by 2019 Vox is the third largest party in the national Congress; its rise mirrors the other half of Spain's political fragmentation — Podemos on the left, Vox on the right.
entries.note: Vox Andalusian result December 2018: Junta de Andalucía official results. National Congress results April and November 2019: Ministerio del Interior. Turnbull-Dugarte (2019), 'Explaining the Emergence of the Extreme Right in Spain', Government and Opposition: the first academic study of Vox's electoral breakthrough. The party's founding: 2013 (breakaway from PP) — documented. The 'Spanish exceptionalism' thesis: Gunther et al. (2004), Democracy in Modern Spain: the pre-Vox academic consensus that Spain had uniquely avoided far-right representation.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: The Spanish economic miracle, boom and crash

entries.label: Tourism — Spain's structural dependency and its contradictions
entries.loc: Spain (Costa del Sol; Barcelona; Canary Islands; Balearics)
entries.hint: Spain is the world's second most visited country (after France) and second largest tourism earner — 12% of GDP, 13% of employment; the tourist model transforms the Mediterranean coast from 1960 onwards; by 2024 anti-tourism protests erupt in Barcelona, the Canary Islands and Mallorca documenting the contradictions of structural dependency on a single sector.
entries.note: Tourism statistics: INE and Turespaña annual reports — primary data. The 83 million visitor figure (2019, pre-COVID record): Ministerio de Industria, Comercio y Turismo. Tourism's share of GDP: Cuenta Satélite del Turismo en España (INE). The anti-tourism protests (Canary Islands, April 2024; Barcelona, July 2024): documented in press and municipal records. Ioannides & Debbage (1998), The Economic Geography of the Tourist Industry: contextualises Spain's model globally. The 1960s origins: documented in Ministerio de Información y Turismo promotional records (AGA).

entries.label: Inditex/Zara — the fast fashion logistics revolution
entries.hint: Amancio Ortega's Zara (1975) develops the most studied supply chain innovation in modern business — design to store in three weeks; 500 new designs weekly; real-time sales data feeding instant production decisions; vertical integration from design to retail; Inditex becomes the world's largest fashion retailer by 2012; the model transforms global retail logistics.
entries.note: Inditex founding documents: Registro Mercantil de A Coruña. The three-week cycle: documented in Inditex annual reports (available from 2001, Comisión Nacional del Mercado de Valores). Ghemawat & Nueno (2003), ZARA: Fast Fashion (Harvard Business School Case 703-497): the foundational business school study. The vertical integration model: documented in CNMV filings. Inditex surpasses GAP as world's largest fashion retailer (2012): financial records. Ortega became Spain's richest person (Forbes 2012): documented.

entries.label: España vaciada — rural depopulation and the two Spains
entries.loc: Interior provinces: Soria; Teruel; Cuenca; Zamora; Guadalajara
entries.hint: Spain empties from the inside — the interior provinces (Soria has the lowest population density in the EU, Teruel 'existe') haemorrhage population to Madrid, Barcelona and the coasts; the 'España vaciada' (emptied Spain) political movement coalesces around the 2019 general elections demanding rural infrastructure investment; a structural demographic crisis with no equivalent in Western Europe.
entries.note: Population density data: INE (Instituto Nacional de Estadística) census — Soria at 8.8 inhabitants/km² (2021), the lowest in the EU. The 'Teruel Existe' movement: founded 1999, won a Congress seat in November 2019 elections — Junta Electoral Central records. The España vaciada manifesto (31 Mar 2019): published rally in Madrid attended by 100,000 people — documented in press. Camarero (2017), La España rural: the standard sociological study. The 2019–2022 parliamentary platform: documented in Congreso records.

entries.label: Reconversión Industrial — dismantling heavy industry for Europe
entries.hint: Felipe González's government systematically dismantles inefficient state-subsidised heavy industries (coal mines, steel mills, shipyards) to prepare Spain for the EEC; the Reconversión Industrial provokes violent general strikes and riots in industrial towns — Asturian miners' marches, the Sagunto steel crisis (1983) — permanently restructuring the labour landscape and initiating Spain's transition to a service economy.
entries.note: The Reconversión Industrial 1983–1992: BOE legislative series. The Sagunto crisis (1983): the steel mill closure sparks the first major post-transition industrial conflict — documented in press (El País archive) and union records. General strikes: UGT and CCOO records. Malo de Molina (1983), El mercado de trabajo en España: the standard economic study of the period. Salmon (1995), The Modern Spanish Economy: the standard English survey. The EU Structural Fund compensation for reconversión areas: documented in EU OJEU records.

entries.label: Spain joins the European Community — 1986
entries.hint: Spain's EU accession on 1 January 1986 ends nearly a decade of negotiations — it triggers massive structural funds, foreign investment and economic integration; Spain's GDP per capita rises from 70% of EU average (1985) to 90% (2007).
entries.note: Accession Treaty signed 12 Jun 1985: Archivo del Ministerio de Asuntos Exteriores (Madrid). Entered into force 1 Jan 1986: Official Journal of the European Communities, L302, 15 Nov 1985. GDP convergence data: Eurostat. The accession negotiations (1977–1985): documented in AMAE. Fuentes Quintana (1989) on the economic consequences.

entries.label: Euro introduction — Spain adopts the single currency (2002)
entries.hint: Spain adopts euro coins and notes on 1 January 2002 — the peseta, in circulation since 1868, is withdrawn; the Euro is the most visible daily sign of European integration; Spain meets the Maastricht convergence criteria through significant fiscal adjustment; the single currency later constrains Spain's response to the 2008 crisis (no devaluation possible).
entries.note: Euro launch 1 Jan 2002: European Central Bank records. The peseta's history: Banco de España archive. Spain's Maastricht convergence (1997–1999): documented in Ministerio de Economía reports. Pérez-García et al. (2006), El euro en España: the standard economic study. The ECB's no-devaluation constraint during the 2008 crisis: documented in Banco de España reports and IMF Article IV consultations with Spain. The peseta exchange rate (166.386 pesetas = 1 euro): fixed 1 Jan 1999, documented.

entries.label: 2008 financial crisis — Spain's property collapse
entries.hint: Spain's property bubble — the largest in the world per capita at its peak — collapses in 2008; unemployment reaches 27% (2013); the EU-IMF bailout of €100bn (2012) is the largest bank rescue in Spanish history; 400,000 mortgage evictions documented 2008–2014.
entries.note: Eviction data: Consejo General del Poder Judicial (CGPJ) annual reports — 400,000+ between 2008 and 2014. Unemployment peak 27%: INE, Encuesta de Población Activa Q1 2013. EU bank rescue memorandum, Jul 2012: European Stability Mechanism records. Salmon (2010), The Modern Spanish Economy: the standard economic history, updated. The political consequences: rise of Podemos and Ciudadanos documented in electoral records from 2015.

entries.label: Housing affordability crisis — Spain's defining issue of the 2020s
entries.hint: Spain's housing crisis becomes the most politically dominant domestic issue by 2023–2025 — rental prices rising 50–100% in major cities since 2015; the 2023 Housing Law introduces rent caps; mass protests ('we can't afford to live here') draw hundreds of thousands in 2024.
entries.note: Rental price data: Idealista and INE housing statistics — documented annual increases. Housing Law (Ley de Vivienda): BOE 25 May 2023. The April 2024 housing protests: documented in El País, RTVE — 100,000+ in Barcelona alone. The Airbnb/tourist flat effect on rental markets: Idealista data, municipal reports from Barcelona and Madrid. The issue's political salience: CIS monthly barometer 2023–2024 shows housing as top concern for the first time. Noteworthy: this is ongoing — uncertainty must be flagged.

categories.label: Technology
categories.subtitle: Scientific and technological developments in democratic Spain

entries.label: Renewable energy transformation — Spain leads the EU
entries.loc: Spain (nationwide; wind: Galicia, Castile; solar: Andalusia, Extremadura)
entries.hint: Spain becomes one of the world's renewable energy leaders — wind power (Iberdrola is the world's largest renewables company by 2020) and solar provide over 50% of Spain's electricity by 2022; in April 2023 Spain runs entirely on renewables for the first time; the transition is driven by EU climate policy, geography and major domestic investment.
entries.note: Red Eléctrica de España (REE) data: Spain's renewable share reaches 50.3% in 2022 — REE annual report (public). The 100% renewable milestone (April 2023): REE real-time data, documented. Iberdrola as world's largest renewables company: financial records and Forbes rankings. Spain's wind capacity: GWEC (Global Wind Energy Council) annual reports. The EU Renewable Energy Directive (2009, 2018): the legislative framework. Escribano (2021), 'Spain's Green New Deal': the standard policy analysis.

entries.label: Severo Ochoa Centre network and the CSIC — Spanish science post-transition
entries.hint: The designation of Severo Ochoa Centres of Excellence (2008 onwards) marks Spain's post-transition investment in scientific infrastructure — from 0.3% of GDP on R&D in 1975 to over 1.4% by 2007; the CSIC (Consejo Superior de Investigaciones Científicas) is the fourth-largest research institution in the EU; Spain's integration into the European Research Area is the scientific dimension of EU membership.
entries.note: Severo Ochoa programme: Spanish Ministry of Science (MICINN) — official documentation from 2008. CSIC institutional profile: CSIC annual reports. R&D spending as % of GDP: INE series 1975–2010 — documents the post-transition trajectory. Spain's ERC grant acquisition: European Research Council data. The austerity cuts to R&D (2010–2014): documented in MICINN budget records — a significant setback. Sanz-Menéndez & Cruz-Castro (2019), 'Research Organisations in Spain' in Research Policy: the standard study.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: European integration, immigration and global Spain

entries.label: NATO membership — 1982 accession and 1986 referendum
entries.hint: Spain joins NATO in 1982 under Calvo-Sotelo — then González holds a referendum on remaining (1986) having promised withdrawal; the 'Yes to NATO' campaign reverses González's own position; Spain stays in NATO (52.5% yes); the episode reveals the Socialists' European integration logic.
entries.note: NATO accession 30 May 1982: NATO records. Referendum 12 Mar 1986: Interior Ministry results (52.5% yes, 39.8% no, 7.7% blank). The campaign's reversal: documented in PSOE internal records and press. The three-condition 'yes': documented in González's statement (BOE). Spain's full military integration (joining the integrated command): 1999 — NATO records.

entries.label: EU Cohesion Funds — the infrastructure of European integration
entries.hint: Spain is the largest net recipient of EU Cohesion and Structural Funds for decades — this massive capital transfer finances the world's second-largest high-speed rail network (AVE), motorway construction and port modernisation; the EU funds physically integrate Spain's transport exchange into the European core, transforming a country still partly isolated by its wider railway gauge.
entries.note: EU Cohesion Fund transfers to Spain: European Commission DG REGIO — Spain's cumulative receipts documented. The AVE network (first line Madrid-Seville 1992): RENFE records; now 3,600+ km. The motorway construction: Ministerio de Fomento historical statistics. Tortella & García Ruiz (2013), Spanish Money and Banking: contextualises the EU fund impact. The irony: EU funds build the standard-gauge AVE that finally integrates Spain, compensating for the 1855 gauge decision documented in the previous folio.

entries.label: Spanish multinationals in Latin America — BBVA, Santander, Telefónica
entries.hint: From the mid-1990s, Spanish multinationals (BBVA, Santander, Telefónica, Repsol, Iberdrola, Endesa) become the dominant foreign investors across Latin America — capturing privatised state enterprises; by 2000 Spain is the second largest foreign investor in the region after the US; the expansion transforms Spain from aid recipient to global investor within one generation.
entries.note: Spanish FDI in Latin America statistics: Banco de España balance of payments data. Santander's acquisition of Banco Santander Chile, Banco Río (Argentina), Banespa (Brazil): documented in CNMV filings. Telefónica's acquisition of Tele-comunicações de São Paulo (Telesp, 1998): CNMV. Chislett (2003), Spanish Direct Investment in Latin America: the standard study. The reversal after 2001 Argentina crisis: documented in Banco de España reports.

entries.label: Instituto Cervantes (1991) — the political economy of Spanish
entries.hint: The Instituto Cervantes (founded 21 March 1991) is Spain's global language diplomacy instrument — 90+ centres in 45 countries; 500 million Spanish speakers make it the world's second most spoken language by native speakers; the institute certifies DELE qualifications recognised worldwide; Spanish is a $50bn+ global industry.
entries.note: Royal Decree founding Instituto Cervantes 21 Mar 1991: BOE. Annual El español: una lengua viva report: Instituto Cervantes — the primary statistical source for global Spanish use. The 500 million native speaker figure: Instituto Cervantes / British Council comparative studies. The economic value of Spanish: García Delgado, Alonso & Jiménez (2012), El español, lengua global: the standard economic study. DELE certification: documented in Instituto Cervantes annual reports.

entries.label: 1992 — Barcelona Olympics and Seville Expo: Spain's coming-out party
entries.hint: 1992 is Spain's international coming-out moment — the Barcelona Olympics and Seville Universal Exposition held simultaneously symbolise the complete transformation from isolated dictatorship to modern European democracy; Barcelona's Olympic architecture (Montjuïc, Olympic Village) permanently reshapes the city; the Expo's 'Isla de la Cartuja' initiates Seville's infrastructure modernisation.
entries.note: Barcelona 1992 Olympics: Comité Olímpic de Catalunya records; IOC documentation. The Olympic Village redevelopment of the waterfront: Arxiu Municipal de Barcelona — transforms Barcelona's coastal geography. The Seville Expo 1992: Archivo General de Andalucía. The AVE high-speed rail Madrid-Seville: inaugurated for the Expo (April 1992) — RENFE records. Moragas & Botella (eds.) (2002), Barcelona: l'herència dels Jocs: the standard study. The two events together received 100+ million visitors.

entries.label: Mass immigration to Spain — 1990s–2000s
entries.hint: Spain transforms from a country of emigrants to one of the world's fastest-growing immigration destinations — from 500,000 foreign residents (1995) to 5.6 million (2008); Moroccan, Romanian, Ecuadorian and other communities create new social realities documented in census and civil registry.
entries.note: INE Padrón Municipal (municipal register): the primary source — records all residents, citizens or not, by nationality. Data series 1996–2008: INE website. Izquierdo & Cornelius (2012), Controlling Immigration: Spain chapter — the standard academic analysis. The Delegación del Gobierno para la Inmigración y la Emigración annual reports: primary government documentation.

entries.label: Schengen accession (1995) — free movement becomes daily life
entries.hint: Spain joins the Schengen Area on 26 March 1995 — passport-free travel across most of Western Europe becomes lived reality for 40 million Spaniards; for a country that had been diplomatically isolated under Franco and whose citizens needed visas for most of Europe until 1986, Schengen is the most tangible daily expression of European integration.
entries.note: Spain's Schengen accession 26 Mar 1995: Official Journal of the European Communities. The original Schengen Agreement (14 Jun 1985): Luxembourg. The contrast with the Franco era (Spanish passports required visas for most Western European countries): documented in Foreign Ministry records. The tourism impact: documented in Frontur statistics (INE). EU enlargement and Schengen's expansion: documented in subsequent accession protocols.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Democratic Spain in its European and global context

entries.label: Prestige oil spill — Nunca Máis
entries.hint: The tanker Prestige sinks off the Galician coast releasing 77,000 tonnes of oil — the worst environmental disaster in Spanish history; the government's delayed response ('the black thread moves away') provokes the Nunca Máis (Never Again) movement and hundreds of thousands of volunteers.
entries.note: The sinking: 13 Nov 2002 (split and sank 19 Nov). Official investigation: Ministerio de Fomento records. Environmental damage: documented in Xunta de Galicia and CSIC (Spanish National Research Council) studies. Volunteer numbers: estimated 300,000+ — documented in contemporary press and municipal records. The 'hilo negro' (black thread) quote by Environment Minister Clos: press archive 18 Nov 2002. Criminal proceedings: Tribunal Supremo ruling 2013. The Nunca Máis movement: its manifesto and records archived at Consello da Cultura Galega.

entries.label: Perejil Island Crisis (2002) — Spain's southern frontier
entries.loc: Perejil Island (Parsley Island) — Strait of Gibraltar
entries.hint: Moroccan troops briefly occupy the tiny uninhabited island of Perejil in the Strait of Gibraltar — Spain responds with military force to expel them (Operation Romeo Sierra); US Secretary of State Powell mediates the resolution; the first post-Cold War territorial military confrontation between a European power and a North African state highlights Spain's unresolved southern geopolitical tensions.
entries.note: The Moroccan occupation 11 Jul 2002: documented in Spanish and Moroccan press. Spain's military response (Operation Romeo Sierra) 17 Jul 2002: Spanish Ministry of Defence official statement. US mediation by Colin Powell: US State Department records. Gillespie (2006), Spain and the Mediterranean: the standard study of Spain-Morocco relations. The crisis's context: Spain's ongoing sovereignty disputes with Morocco (Ceuta, Melilla, Alhucemas) — documented in Foreign Ministry records. The island returns to its undefined status after the crisis — neither sovereign state acknowledges the other's claim.

entries.label: 15-F anti-Iraq War protests — largest in Spanish history
entries.hint: Between 10 and 11 million Spaniards protest against the Iraq War on 15 February 2003 — making Spain the country with the highest per-capita anti-war turnout globally; Aznar's support for the war despite 90% public opposition becomes the defining political wound of his second term.
entries.note: Turnout estimates: Interior Ministry (650,000 in Madrid alone); organisers (2 million Madrid). National total: 10–11 million (organisers); widely cited as largest protest in Spanish history. Aznar's 'Letter of Eight' (WSJ, 30 Jan 2003): the primary document of his Iraq position. Public opinion: CIS survey Feb 2003 — 91% of Spaniards opposed the war (CIS study 2481). The protest's connection to the 11-M electoral result: argued in press, documented in CIS post-election surveys.

entries.label: Aznar joins the Iraq War coalition — and 11-M changes the government
entries.hint: Aznar joins Bush and Blair at the Azores summit (16 March 2003) backing the Iraq invasion against overwhelming Spanish public opposition (15-F protests, 10 million march); three days before the 2004 election, al-Qaeda bombs Madrid's commuter trains killing 192; the PP government's misattribution of the attack to ETA (documented in real time) is widely seen as the decisive factor in the PSOE's unexpected election victory three days later.
entries.note: The Azores summit 16 Mar 2003: documented in White House and Moncloa press records. The 11-M bombings 11 Mar 2004: AHN and the Congressional Commission of Investigation (2005) — the definitive primary investigation. The PP's ETA attribution: documented in real-time press releases (Moncloa 11 Mar 2004) and contradicted within hours by police evidence. Jordán (2014), España en la 'guerra contra el terror': the standard study. Zapatero's troop withdrawal announced election night: documented.

entries.label: COVID-19 pandemic — Spain among the worst hit
entries.hint: Spain is among the first European countries overwhelmed by COVID-19 — Madrid's hospitals collapse in March 2020; Spain declares a state of alarm (14 March), one of Europe's strictest lockdowns; official deaths exceed 120,000 by mid-2021; the pandemic triggers a €140bn EU recovery fund allocation.
entries.note: State of alarm decree: Real Decreto 463/2020, BOE 14 Mar 2020. Official death toll: INE and Centro Nacional de Epidemiología data — disputed between official (87,000) and excess mortality estimates (120,000+). EU recovery fund (Plan de Recuperación, Transformación y Resiliencia): BOE and European Commission documentation. The nursing home deaths (residencias de mayores): Defensor del Pueblo inquiry report (2021). The pandemic's political impact: regional competence disputes documented in Constitutional Court records.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: Jose Perez Ocana -- street drag, anarchism and the first protest against the Ley de Peligrosidad (1977)
entries.loc: Barcelona (Las Ramblas; Placa Reial; Park Guell)
entries.hint: Jose Perez Ocana, an Andalusian painter who moves to Barcelona around 1971, becomes the central figure of Las Ramblas' emerging counterculture -- performing in drag daily, fusing Andalusian religious imagery with flamboyant theatricality; he leads the first public demonstration against the Ley de Peligrosidad Social at the 1977 Jornadas Libertarias in Park Guell; Ventura Pons's 1978 documentary Ocana, retrat intermitent captures him discussing the law directly; he dies in 1983 when his costume catches fire during a performance, aged 36. He sits at the earliest point of the queer visibility arc this folio documents, two decades before La Veneno's television breakthrough.
entries.note: Ventura Pons, Ocana, retrat intermitent (1978): extant documentary, Cannes selection -- the primary visual record, includes Ocana discussing the Ley de Peligrosidad Social directly. The 1977 Jornadas Libertarias (CNT, Park Guell): documented in contemporary press and Pons's film. MACBA holds over 200 surviving paintings and papier-mache sculptures -- the primary material collection. Centro de Interpretacion Ocana, Cantillana (opened 2018): institutional documentation of his life and work. His death 18 Sep 1983 (costume fire during a performance in Seville): documented in press. Preciado (2023), 'The Ocana We Deserve', Stedelijk Studies: the standard critical reassessment.

entries.label: Romani community — gitanos in democratic Spain
entries.hint: Spain's 750,000 Romani (gitano) population is the largest in Western Europe — the Constitution forbids ethnic discrimination but structural inequalities are documented in education, housing and employment; the Fundación Secretariado Gitano produces annual reports measuring the gap between legal equality and lived reality.
entries.note: Fundación Secretariado Gitano, Discriminación y Comunidad Gitana: annual reports from 2004 (primary data source). INE population estimates: ~750,000–1 million. The gitano community's legal status under democracy: Constitutional Court jurisprudence on Roma rights. Laparra (2007), La exclusión social en España: the standard academic study. The community's cultural role in flamenco: documented in ethnomusicological literature.

entries.label: The heroin epidemic and the generación perdida (1980s)
entries.hint: Heroin floods Spain from 1979 — arriving through the same criminal networks supplying French markets; in working-class neighbourhoods of Madrid, Barcelona and Bilbao entire peer groups are lost; INE mortality data shows heroin-related deaths peak at 1,855 in 1991; the epidemic disproportionately devastates the children of the post-Franco transition; the cine quinqui documents it in real time.
entries.note: Heroin mortality statistics: INE mortality tables — primary data. Ministerio de Sanidad drug use surveys (EDADES series, from 1995): primary surveillance data. The cine quinqui as primary source: Eloy de la Iglesia's El pico (1983) and El pico 2 (1984) — cast with actual heroin users; the films are documentary evidence. Romaní (1999), Las drogas: sueños y razones: the standard sociological study. The epidemic's geographic concentration in post-industrial working-class neighbourhoods: documented in municipal health records (Madrid and Barcelona).

entries.label: Almería greenhouses — the human cost of Spain's agricultural miracle
entries.loc: El Ejido; Roquetas de Mar (Almería — 'Sea of Plastic')
entries.hint: Tens of thousands of undocumented sub-Saharan African and Moroccan workers sustain the Almería greenhouse economy that supplies much of Europe's winter vegetables — they work in extreme heat with toxic agrochemicals, live in chabolas (makeshift shanties of discarded plastic and wood) without running water or electricity, earning below minimum wage; Europe's largest agricultural labour human rights crisis.
entries.note: The El Ejido riots (February 2000 — attacks by Spanish residents on migrant workers): documented in press and Defensor del Pueblo report. The Almería greenhouse sector economics: INE agricultural statistics — 30,000+ hectares under plastic. Human Rights Watch (2000), Memorandum on Spain and Migrant Workers: the foundational rights report on Almería conditions. Checa (2001), El Ejido, la ciudad-cortijo: the standard sociological study. The chabola communities: documented in Médicos del Mundo and SOS Racismo reports. The Almería model: documented as the direct product of Spain's post-EEC agricultural liberalisation.

entries.label: Murder of Ana Orantes (1997) — the case that changed Spain
entries.hint: Ana Orantes, 60, goes on Andalusian television (Canal Sur) to recount forty years of domestic abuse — a legal system treating domestic violence as a private matter forces her to share a home with her abuser despite divorce; thirteen days later he burns her alive; the murder shatters Spain's silence on domestic violence and directly triggers the 2004 Comprehensive Law against Gender-Based Violence.
entries.note: Ana Orantes's Canal Sur interview (10 Dec 1997): Canal Sur archive. Her murder 17 Dec 1997: documented in police and judicial records. Her husband José Parejo's conviction: Supreme Court. The 2004 Ley Orgánica de Medidas de Protección Integral contra la Violencia de Género (28 Dec 2004): BOE — explicitly influenced by the Orantes case in parliamentary debate. Bosch et al. (2013), Femicidio/Feminicidio: analyses the Orantes case as the legislative catalyst. This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT: the Orantes murder transforms domestic violence from a private family matter into a public constitutional emergency in Spanish law.

entries.label: Same-sex marriage legalised — 2005
entries.hint: Spain legalises same-sex marriage on 30 June 2005 — the third country in the world after the Netherlands and Belgium; Prime Minister Zapatero explicitly frames it as completing the democratic revolution begun in 1978; the Constitutional Court upholds it in 2012.
entries.note: Ley 13/2005: BOE 2 Jul 2005 — the law text. The Congressional vote (30 Apr 2005): 183 in favour, 136 against — Diario de Sesiones del Congreso. Constitutional Court Ruling STC 198/2012 (6 Nov 2012): upholds the law 8–3. Spain was the third country globally and the first majority-Catholic country. The law's social context: CIS (Centro de Investigaciones Sociológicas) surveys document public opinion shifts 1985–2005.

entries.label: Mileuristas — Spain's precarious educated generation
entries.loc: Spain (nationwide; Madrid and Barcelona most acute)
entries.hint: In 2005 a letter to El País coins 'mileurista' — a highly educated, multilingual graduate trapped in precarious temporary contracts earning barely €1,000/month; the 2008 crash sends youth unemployment past 55%; the 'lost generation' is defined by delayed independence, declining birth rates, and mass emigration of Spain's most educated youth to northern Europe.
entries.note: The mileurista letter (El País, 21 Aug 2005): El País archive — the coinage by Carolina Alguacil. Youth unemployment statistics (2013 peak, 55.7%): Eurostat — the highest in the EU at the time. The emigration data: INE Estadística de Variaciones Residenciales — documented surge of 25–34 year olds to Germany, UK, France 2008–2015. Muñoz Molina (2013), Todo lo que era sólido: a literary primary source on generational precarity. The housing affordability crisis entry (s3) documents the structural continuation of mileurista precarity.

entries.label: 8-M feminist strikes — 2018 and 2019
entries.hint: Spain's 8 March feminist strikes (2018, 2019) are the largest women's mobilisations in Spanish history — 5.3 million march in 2018 according to organisers; the strikes produce Spain's most intense public debate on gender violence, equal pay and political representation.
entries.note: Strike turnout: Delegación del Gobierno para la Violencia de Género data; police counts lower (120,000 in Madrid alone). The Manifiesto 8-M (2018): published in full in major press. The legal debate around the strike's legality: Audiencia Nacional rulings. The subsequent 'purple tide' legislation — Ley Orgánica de Violencia de Género was already in place (LO 1/2004: BOE 29 Dec 2004) — but the 2018 strikes accelerated the Ley del Solo Sí es Sí (sexual consent law, 2022).

entries.label: Gender identity law — 2023
entries.hint: Spain passes the 'Trans Law' (Ley 4/2023 para la igualdad real y efectiva de las personas trans) allowing legal gender change by self-declaration without medical requirements — one of the most progressive gender identity laws in Europe; passed despite internal PSOE opposition.
entries.note: Ley 4/2023: BOE 2 Mar 2023. The congressional vote (188 in favour, 150 against, 12 abstentions): Diario de Sesiones, 16 Feb 2023. The internal PSOE debate: documented in party records and press. The contrasting debate between trans rights advocates and some feminist groups: documented in El País and Público archives 2020–2023. This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT: from medicalised gender identity to self-declaration.

entries.label: La Veneno and Pedro Zerolo — visibility and the politics of LGBTQ+ identity
entries.hint: Cristina Ortiz 'La Veneno' — a transgender sex worker from Adra (Almería) who becomes a television celebrity on late-night talk shows in 1996 — is the first trans figure with mass popular visibility in Spain; Pedro Zerolo (PSOE councillor, grandson of a Canarian Republican exile) is the architect of Spain's 2005 same-sex marriage law and the face of its parliamentary campaign; both embody the transition from marginalisation to legal recognition.
entries.note: La Veneno's first television appearance: Esta noche cruzamos el Mississipi (Telecinco, 1996) — documented in TVE and Telecinco archives. Her biography: Valeria Vegas (2016), Libérate: the primary biographical account. Her death 5 Nov 2016: documented. The TV series Veneno (Atresplayer, 2020): documentary-drama that restored her legacy. Pedro Zerolo's parliamentary role in the same-sex marriage law (Ley 13/2005): Congreso de los Diputados debates — his speech of 30 June 2005. Zerolo's death 9 Jun 2015 (cancer): documented. These two figures together document the arc from criminalization to constitutional protection.
